entry.title: How SAP ESM + Document AI + S/4HANA Work Together to Automate Invoice Processing End‑to‑End
entry.content:
One of the most powerful capabilities in SAP Enterprise Service Management (ESM) is its ability to automate invoice processing the moment a customer or vendor sends an email. With SAP Document Information Extraction (Document AI) integrated into ESM, the journey from a raw invoice PDF to a validated posting in SAP S/4HANA Private or Public Edition becomes almost fully automated.
Click on Image to view High Quality
Click on Image to view High Quality

Step 1: The User Sends an Email with an Invoice

A customer or vendor sends an email to the SAP ESM dedicated email address configured for automatic case creation.

The invoice may be:

  • Attached as a PDF, JPEG, PNG, or
  • Embedded in the email body itself

SAP ESM picks up both.

Step 2: SAP ESM Automatically Creates a Case

Once the email reaches the ESM mailbox:

  • A new case is created automatically
  • The email body and all attachments are stored within the case
  • The attachments (PDFs, images) trigger SAP Document AI processing immediately

This eliminates manual case creation entirely.

Step 3: SAP Document AI Processes the Invoice

SAP Document AI retrieves the attachment from the case and begins extraction using OCR and machine learning.

It identifies:

  • Invoice number
  • Customer or vendor name
  • Address
  • Line items
  • Totals & taxes
  • Payment terms
  • Contract or PO references

Document AI also calculates a confidence score for each extracted field.
These scores help determine whether the data is reliable enough to auto‑post or requires user intervention.

Step 4: Validation Using Business Rules & Customer Framework

Once extraction is complete, SAP ESM applies:

  • Business rules
  • Customer validation frameworks
  • Mapping logic
  • Master data checks

These validations are essential before communicating with S/4HANA.

Examples:

  • Is the customer known in S/4?
  • Is the invoice number valid and not duplicated?
  • Does the PO exist?
  • Do line item totals match?

Only when the validations pass does ESM proceed to the next step.

Step 5: Invoice Number & Customer Auto‑Identification

If Document AI finds the invoice number, SAP ESM updates the line items and case fields:

  • invoiceNumber → updated
  • customerNumber → auto‑identified
  • isClearing → set according to logic (e.g., invoice found, ready to post)

This gives the ESM user immediate visibility:

  • Has the system recognized the invoice?
  • Has the customer been identified?
  • Is the invoice ready for posting?

Step 6: ESM Sends the Validated Invoice to S/4HANA via OData APIs

Once the case has all mandatory and validated data, the ESM user can trigger the next action:

“Post Invoice to S/4”

At this point:

  • ESM calls S/4HANA OData APIs
  • A draft or final FI invoice is created
  • Additional steps like clearing, matching, or workflow approvals occur inside S/4

This hands-off process ensures:

  • No manual entry in S/4
  • No data inconsistencies
  • Full auditability from source → document → posting

Step 7: ESM User Monitors and Completes the Process

Inside the ESM case, the user can now see:

  • Status of the posting
  • Document number returned from S/4
  • Any errors (e.g., master data inconsistencies)
  • New actions available for follow-up

They can then:

  • Update the customer
  • Add missing reference information
  • Re-trigger the posting
  • Close the case once processing is complete

Result: A Fully Streamlined, AI‑Driven Invoice Workflow

By integrating SAP ESM, Document AI, and S/4HANA, organizations benefit from:

✔ Zero manual invoice entry

✔ Auto-identification of customers and invoice data

✔ Early error detection using business rules

✔ Faster posting to S/4HANA

✔ End-to-end transparency in one central case

✔ Higher accuracy and reduced effort

This modernizes invoice handling from a traditionally manual process into an intelligent, automated workflow.

Learning Journey :  Business Documents in ESM

entry.title: SAP Enterprise Service Management [ESM] - How to enable Business Document
entry.content:
Previous Blog : How SAP ESM + Document AI + S/4HANA Work Together to Automate Invoice Processing End‑to‑End

Business Documents

Business Document feature available in SAP Enterprise Service Management SKU (ESM)

Business Documents represent back-end records in a semantic way and can create, update, or display those records. Values in the table can be populated automatically using AI services or entered manually.

Business documents can capture data from email message or case subject and description, attachments of various file types, or using manual data entry. Attachment data and entity information is extracted with generative AI using large language models.

You, as an administrator, can create business document schemas to represent data patterns for different scenarios. Schemas specify which data to use in your business documents and how you want the data formatted in your solution. If you've enabled business documents for a specific case type, you can find the Business Documents tab on the case detail view. Use conditions to make specific business document fields required, read only, or hidden.

Business Document Integration

Create an outbound configuration to enable actions to send or retrieve data from external systems, or to populate values in dropdown menus in business documents based on a dynamic data source.

Prerequisites

Set up an outbound communication system under System Settings -  Integration  - Communication Systems.

Procedure

Go to your user menu and choose System Settings  - Business Documents - Business Document Integration.

  • Choose + (Create).
  • Enter a name and select the communication system.
  • Choose Search and Add to add existing HTTP data outbound configurations or + (Create) to create a new configuration.

Create Business Documents

Select + Create Business Document, Enter the required information, and save

Your new business document appears in the Business Documents list with the header details on the right. A new draft version of the business document appears in the Versions table. Select + Actions to activate the version, copy to a new business document, create new version, or delete the version. There can only be one draft version. You can activate multiple versions but there must only be one active version within the specified validity period.

Select + in the Designer tab to add controls and fields to the business document table.

Add actions to the business document. Go to the Actions tab and select + Add Action.

Go to the Input Options tab to choose one or more input methods for your business document.

Configure Business Document Extraction Schemas

Use schemas to configure document extraction from attachments.

Add Business Document to Case Type

  1. Go to User Menu > System Settings  > All Settings  > Cases  > Case Types.
  2. Select a case type.
  3. Select  (Open in Detail View) to open the case type details.
  4. Switch on Business Documents in the Features Settings section

  1. Choose the Additional Features tab in the case type header.
  2. In the Business Documents section, choose the  (Search) icon to search for a business document to add to this case type.
  3. Choose + (Add) to add the business document to the case type.

    You can assign only one business document to each case type. When a case of this type is created, the Business Documents tab appears in the case detail view.

entry.title: Automate Documents End-to-End with SAP Document AI (Beginner’s Guide)
entry.content:
Introduction:

SAP Document AI is a Business Technology Platform (BTP) service designed to automate extraction of information from diverse business documents, such as invoices, regardless of their layout or content structure. It is available in Cloud Foundry and Kyma environments. To set up SAP Document AI in a free BTP trial account, users need an S-user ID to create a trial BTP Trial global account and sub-account. Check this link for more information.

Below are the setup steps and example about Sap Document AI-

SAP Document AI is available in the following environments:

  • Cloud Foundry Environment
  • Kyma environment

 Setup steps:

Get started with SAP Document AI using the standard procedures for SAP BTP Cloud Foundry environment or Kyma environment.

Prerequisites to set up SAP Document AI in BTP trial account-

1.Required S-user id for BTP Trial account creation.

 

BTP Trial account login screen

 

2.Select the Region and create Sub-account in BTP Global account

 

3.In Sub-account add the “SAP document AI” Subscription and instance.

After adding the instance and subscription of SAP Document AI. Go to Subscription three dots and click on Go to Application Option.

Example- Now you will get the SAP Document AI subscription click this then UI will open. Here you will get the option to upload the invoice.

Below steps for uploading documents in SAP Document AI -

  1. In the top right, click + (Upload Document).

2.On the Select Document screen, choose the document type from drop down list -Invoice, Payment Advice, Purchase Order, Custom.

3.Next, select the Schema.

You must select a schema when you add documents. By default, SAP Document AI proposes the appropriate preconfigured SAP schema for your document type. To see the header and line-item fields that are available in an SAP schema, choose the desired Schema on the Select Document screen, and then choose Extraction Fields to display the schema details.

For Upload the document in this page click on + icon and upload the invoice add the document from system and click on confirm.

In Below screenshot document type is Invoice and Schema type is SAP Invoice Schema.

After uploading Save and Confirm check the status is pending or done of uploaded document.

After successful upload of the document in “SAP Document AI” data extraction process will start, and it will show the extraction confidence range like red, yellow and green status with accuracy percentage.

Handling Warnings and Errors in Invoice Data:

The invoice below displays warnings and errors in the data. SAP Document AI allows you to easily identify these issues, providing an efficient way to review and correct errors and warnings in extracted invoice information. This feature enhances the analysis and accuracy of invoice data by enabling users to update any incorrect entries after extraction.

Warning data- Click on Extraction warning confidence range and check the warning data if required changes update and confirm.

When the extracted data from the invoice is updated correctly, it is indicated by a green status. This green status signifies that the information has been accurately captured and no further corrections are necessary, confirming the integrity and reliability of the invoice data.

Error in Invoice-

Errors may occur in invoices during the document upload and extraction process in SAP Document AI. These errors can impact the accuracy and reliability of the extracted data, making it necessary to review and correct them to ensure proper invoice processing.

When you add documents, you need to choose a schema. SAP Document AI will automatically suggest a suitable SAP schema based on your document type. To view the available header and line-item fields in the chosen schema, simply select the Schema on the Select Document screen, and then click on Extraction Fields to see the details.

Reviewing and Updating Errors or Warnings

If the uploaded invoice contains errors or warnings, check them in the Extraction Confidence Range. The errors and warnings are indicated by specific color codes. Make the necessary changes to the invoice data and save your updates. Once saved, the invoice data and the confidence range color codes for errors and warnings will be updated to reflect the corrections.

Correcting Invoice Information

For example, in the invoice below, the Bill To section displays the Client Name and Address as not correctly updated in the Extraction Confidence Range. This indicates that these fields require review and adjustment to ensure accurate data extraction and confidence scoring.

Steps to Correct Invoice Data:

Step1: Go to edit mode.

When the edit button is selected, all the invoice data will change to a gray shade. This indicates that the fields are now in editable mode, allowing you to make the necessary corrections.

Step 2: Click on the Name and Address fields in the invoice.

These fields can now be updated as needed to correct any errors that were identified during the data extraction process. Enter the correct field value and then click on "Apply" to save your changes to that field.

Once you have saved and confirmed your changes, the error will be resolved. The extraction value will be updated and displayed in green, indicating that the correction has been successfully applied and the data is now considered accurate.

Conclusion: 

This document outlines the process for identifying and correcting errors in uploaded invoice data using the Extraction Confidence Range. SAP Document AI is smart tool for organizations to automate document processing with accuracy and speed, reducing manual effort and errors. By leveraging AI and seamless SAP integration, it given smarter, scalable, and more efficient business processes.

entry.title: From Hype to Hands-On: Level Up Your AI Skills This February
entry.content:
In the age of AI, the skills we build today will define how we work tomorrow. That’s why we’ve launched a monthly blog series that spotlights the latest AI learning assets and upcoming learning opportunities.

Building on my first blog, where I introduced you to our AI live sessions Beyond Hype (which is all about getting productive with SAP Business AI), I wanted to address some of your most asked questions:

  1. What is the difference between Joule skills and Joule agents?

    Joule skills are designed for executing single, predefined tasks independently. These skills enhance SAP's default capabilities by leveraging unstructured inputs to perform specific, context-aware actions, such as data retrieval or system transactions, in a deterministic, predictable manner. Joule Skills integrates with both SAP and third-party APIs to automate and streamline operations such as ticket creation and workflow execution, all initiated through conversational interactions.

    Joule Agents are AI applications that assist business users with decision support and task automation. They use LLMs and reasoning models to determine actions and their sequence, addressing various situations autonomously. Joule Agents can autonomously execute complex multi-step business processes. They engage users to review and approve significant actions that impact the business, such as extending discounts to dissatisfied customers. User requests or specific business events, such as the creation of a new sales order or customer service ticket, can trigger Joule Agents.

    Deep dive by watching this video: What are Joule agents and skills?  

  2. How can we start from scratch with Joule Studio to create our own portfolio of Joule skills and Joule agents?

    To use Joule Studio, the SAP Build developer license (SKU 8019311) is required, and you need to install the SAP Build Process Automation service to leverage the Cloud Integration Automation service scenario for SAP Build. Please note that you need to be on the new unified SAP Build commercial model.

    And of course, you need Joule Base or Premium. Please note, Joule Studio works with any Joule brownfield license, except Joule for Consultants and Joule for Developers. To run custom Joule Skills in your production environments, you do not need AI Units. However, execution of custom Joule Agents will consume AI Units.

    To all Partners, a TDD license is available on the SAP Partner Portal! Read on to learn how to set up Joule Studio for all partner flows and entitlement levels. I am excited to share the link to the free 30-day basic trial. Request now and select workbook: Create a Joule Agent with Joule Studio!

    And lastly, here are the instructions to set up Joule Studio for all customer flows and entitlement levels.

  3. Besides building low-code and no-code agents with Joule Studio, can I also code them in Python?

    Of course, you can! Have a look at the SAP Community Blog Post by @noravonthenen  from the Developer Advocates. In her article, she shares her experience building an AI Agent with LiteLLM, CrewAI, and SAP-RPT-1, and what actually worked. You can also check out this GitHub repository and learn how to Build Your Own AI Agent-Based Solution using the SAP Cloud SDK for AI and the Generative AI Hub in AI Foundation.

However, let’s continue to focus on Joule Studio. In this three-part tutorial series, we will walk you through the complete journey of building your own custom AI agent in Joule Studio, from defining the business use case to extending its capabilities and finally bringing it to life.

Each part builds on the previous one. By the end, you won’t just understand the theory, you’ll have created a fully functional agent tailored to your scenario.

  1.  Define the use case: We start by grounding everything in a concrete scenario for Intelligent Maintenance Order Fulfillment. What problem are we solving? What outcome are we aiming for? This foundation ensures the agent is built with purpose.

     

  2. Extend the Joule Skill: Next, we move from concept to capability. You’ll learn how to extend an existing skill to support the custom requirements of your use case.

     

  3. Create the Joule Agent: Finally, we assemble everything into a working agent — connecting the extended skill and configuring the behavior so it can operate autonomously.

     

Want to dive deeper into the content? Rewatch the session and access the session material via SAP Learning Hub (subscription required) and explore your key resources to help you get started building your own Joule agents:

  • Step-by-step guidance to build custom Joule agents in Joule Studio (recording)
  • Joule Studio Goes Live: Answers to Your Top Questions covering Skills and the new Agent Builder features 
  • Best Practices for Building Agents: Key principles and practices for designing effective Joule Agents 
  • Discovery Center Mission: Get Hands-on experience in creating a Joule Agent with multiple skills and MCP
  • Since you also asked about a certification for Joule Studio, the SAP Build Developer Certification includes Joule Studio.

Hi Joule, where should I get started with the latest learning resources? *

Apart from live enablement sessions, get hands-on with our latest on-demand learning resources:

  • Build AI services using the SAP Cloud Application Programming Model, SAP AI Core, and Generative AI Hub.
  • Explore how to make Large Language Models truly enterprise-ready by grounding them with your own business data using Document Grounding in SAP Generative AI Hub.
  • Create your own schemas from scratch in the SAP Document AI workspace. Go ahead and try it yourself! 
  • Learn how to deploy an LLM on SAP AI Core and query it via SAP AI Launchpad, the orchestration service, and the Python SDK.
  • Get started with Machine Learning using SAP HANA and Python learn how the Machine Learning process develops using a Python Client for SAP HANA.
  • Get started with AI (Vector Engine, NLP, Knowledge Graph[soon]) using SAP HANA and Python, and learn how SAP HANA Cloud supports the development of generative AI applications.
  • Implement enterprise-grade AI solutions using SAP's AI services and integration with generative AI models.

 As you can see, some SAP Samples were used for an SAP CodeJam. An SAP CodeJam is a free, hands-on developer workshop organized by SAP that focuses on a specific technology topic. It’s designed for developers who want practical, guided experience — not just slides, but real coding. If you want to experience a SAP CodeJam in person, check out the upcoming events in your region and sign up!

* Powered by the machine learning technology of SAP AI Business Services, personalized recommendations are generated for employees, in one place on the Learning home page, based on employee data.

As we look ahead, we’re excited to share with you a new lineup of learning opportunities designed to help you expand your knowledge and make AI an advantage in your role:

  • March 5, 11 am CET: Accelerate your transformation to the Cloud with SAP Business AI. Discover how SAP’s AI-powered migration toolchain enables customers to accelerate their transformation with less risk and cost to the SAP Business Suite.
  • March 12, 11 am CET: Efficient Document Inbound Processing in SAP Cloud ERP with SAP Document AI. Explore the pre-delivered document inbound processing use cases for SAP Cloud ERP across Sales, Finance, and Quality Management.
  • March 19, 10 am CET: All Things Business AI: Latest Insights, Updates & Live Demos. Discover current AI trends, recent updates across SAP’s AI portfolio, and see live demos showcasing how Business AI can drive efficiency and innovation in real-world business processes.

Stay tuned for more, and happy learning! 

Further Resources:

  • From Hype to Hands-On: Level Up Your AI Skills This October
  • From Hype to Hands-On: Level Up Your AI Skills This November
  • From Hype to Hands-On: Level Up Your AI Skills This December
  • From Hype to Hands-On: Level Up Your AI Skills This January

Joule Studio SAP Business AI Joule  SAP Document AI AI Foundation #sapclouderp #handson

entry.title: What happen in SAP Columbus first Physical meet of 2026?
entry.content:
On February 26, 2026, we had our first meeting of this year. It was a face-to-face meeting. We discussed the AI applications in Supply Chain Maangement and Plant Maintenance. We also discussed about the management of sensors in the Utility industry. We did some planning for SAP INSIDE TRACK which we are planning in month of April. After considering all suggestions, we finalized the date for SIT as 25th April. It will be our first SAP INSIDE TRACK in Columbus. 
We also discussed a pre-event in March which will be a rehearsal for SIT. This event is SAP Innovation Festival. I have posted the event and invited guests there.

entry.title: How Invoice Scan Business Documents Transform Efficiency in SAP Enterprise Service Management (ESM)
entry.content:
Previous Article : https://community.sap.com/t5/crm-and-cx-blog-posts-by-sap/sap-enterprise-service-management-esm-how-to-enable-business-document/ba-p/14332556 

In today’s fast‑moving business environment, organizations are under pressure to process vendor invoices faster, more accurately, and with fewer manual steps. Traditional invoice processing—where teams manually enter data, verify suppliers, and route documents—slows down operations, introduces errors, and delays payments.

SAP Enterprise Service Management (ESM) changes the game with the Invoice Scan Business Document framework. This smart document‑processing capability enables companies to digitize invoices, automatically extract key information, and match it with the right customer or business partner from S/4 HANA with SAP ESM

Let’s explore how this works and why it matters.

What Is an Invoice Scan Business Document?

An Invoice Scan Business Document in SAP is a digital representation of an invoice created from a scanned file or an email attachment. Using SAP Document AI (DOCX AI) machine learning and OCR (Optical Character Recognition), SAP DOCX AI extracts structured data from unstructured content—such as:

  • Customer name
  • Address and contact data
  • Invoice number
  • Currency
  • PO number
  • Item details
  • Tax values
  • Payment Reference
  • Payment terms

This scanned document becomes a Business Document inside SAP ESM, which can be processed like any other system-native document.

How Invoice Scanning Speeds Up Processing

1. Automated Data Extraction

Instead of manually keying in vendor or customer details, SAP identifies key fields through:

  • SAP DOCX AI OCR text capture
  • Semantic understanding of invoice layouts
  • Machine learning models trained on global document formats

This eliminates the most time-consuming part of invoice processing: data entry.

2. Faster Customer Identification Using S/4HANA & ESM

One of the biggest challenges in invoice processing is matching the scanned invoice to the correct customer or service contract.

S/4HANA & ESM handles this automatically by comparing extracted information with master data records:

  • Customer name
  • Email domain
  • Contract or service order numbers
  • Address and tax identifiers
  • Contact details

If the system finds a unique match, it automatically assigns the business partner.
If not, it presents the most likely candidates for the processor to confirm.

This drastically reduces:

  • Lookup time
  • Assignment errors
  • Duplicate customer creation

3. Intelligent Error Detection

SAP Document AI automatically flags inconsistencies such as:

  • Missing PO number
  • Mismatched customer name
  • Incorrect totals
  • Expired or mismatched contracts

This allows finance or shared service teams to resolve issues early, not after posting.

4. Seamless Integration with Workflow

Once captured, the invoice can be routed for:

  • Approval
  • Verification
  • Posting
  • Payment

All using SAP Build Process Automation flexible workflow engine.

This enables:

  • End-to-end traceability
  • SLA-based approvals
  • Automatic posting when confidence levels are high

Why This Matters to Finance, Shared Services & ESM Teams

✔ Faster Invoice-to-Pay Cycle

Invoices move through the system more quickly, accelerating payment and improving vendor relationships.

✔ Reduced Manual Effort

Teams spend less time typing and more time analyzing.

✔ Higher Accuracy

Machine learning reduces human errors and improves data consistency.

✔ Better Compliance & Auditability

Every extracted value, system recommendation, and user decision is logged.

✔ Scalable Automation

Once trained, the system improves over time, reducing workload even further.

Example Scenario: Identifying the Customer Automatically

  1. A vendor emails a PDF invoice.
  2. SAP ESM creates a Case & Business Document automatically creates an Invoice Scan Business Document.
  3. SAP DOCX AI (OCR) extracts key data like customer ID, service order, or contract reference.
  4. S/4HANA & ESM compares this to its master data.
  5. The system:
    • Auto-assigns the correct customer when confidence is high, or
    • Suggests the top matches for confirmation.
  6. The invoice is routed for approval or automatically posted if rules allow.

Result:
An end-to-end automated workflow from incoming invoice → validated document → assigned customer → posting.

Conclusion: A Smarter Way to Handle Invoices

The combination of Invoice Scan Business Documents and SAP S/4HANA Public or Private Cloud & SAP ESM represents a major step forward in invoice automation. By intelligently extracting data, identifying the correct customer, and integrating with workflows, organizations can:

  • Cut processing time
  • Improve accuracy
  • Reduce administrative workload
  • Enhance compliance
  • Support strategic finance operations

As businesses continue their digital transformation journeys, smart document processing becomes not just an advantage—but a necessity.

entry.title: SAP AI Core: Authentication Errors and How to Fix Them (Part 1 of 2)
entry.content:
SAP COMMUNITY  |  TROUBLESHOOTING  |  SAP AI CORE  |  PART 1 OF 2

SAP AI Core: Authentication Errors and How to Fix Them

Every error between your first curl command and a working bearer token — what causes them, what they actually mean, and the exact fix for each one. Part 1 of 2.

By Deepa Dorairaj   |  SAP AI Solution Architect  |  SAP AI Core Troubleshooting Series  |  Published 2026

TL;DR: Getting your first SAP AI Core bearer token working on Windows is harder than it should be. This article documents every authentication error between zero and a working token — PowerShell curl alias conflicts, quote character issues, port errors, missing OAuth path, empty responses, and RBAC denials — with the exact cause and fix for each. Part 2 covers deployment and configuration errors once authentication is working.

Why This Article Exists

The SAP AI Core documentation covers the happy path well. What it doesn't cover is the sequence of errors most practitioners hit on Windows before they ever get a working API call. These errors are not obscure — they come up consistently across different BTP setups, different teams, and different experience levels.

This article documents six authentication errors in the order they typically appear, based on real troubleshooting sessions. Each error has a clear cause and a specific fix. No generic advice — only what actually works.

Part 2 (Friday) covers what happens after authentication works: 404 on deployment calls, two-instance confusion, resource group provisioning failures, and orchestration vs foundation-models mismatches.

BEFORE YOU START 

What You Need

This guide assumes you have an SAP AI Core service instance on BTP with at least one service key created. From the service key JSON you need four values:

  • url — the OAuth authentication base URL (e.g. https://your-subdomain.authentication.us10.hana.ondemand.com)
  • clientid — your OAuth client ID
  • clientsecret — your OAuth client secret
  • serviceurls.AI_API_URL — your AI Core API base URL

The url field in your service key does NOT include /oauth/token. You must append it manually. This is the single most common cause of authentication failures and is covered in Error 5 below.

ERROR 1 OF 6  
  ERROR 1    PowerShell curl alias — -X not recognized    ParameterBindingException 
What You See
Invoke-WebRequest : A parameter cannot be found that matches parameter name 'X'.
At line:1 char:6
+ curl -X POST 'https://...authentication.../oauth/token'
What Causes It

PowerShell has a built-in alias called curl that actually runs Invoke-WebRequest — Microsoft's own HTTP cmdlet. Invoke-WebRequest uses completely different syntax and does not understand the -X flag that real curl uses. When you type curl in PowerShell you are not running curl.exe — you are running Invoke-WebRequest.

The Fix

Two options — use Command Prompt instead of PowerShell, or explicitly call curl.exe to bypass the alias:

# Option 1 — Open Command Prompt (cmd) instead of PowerShell
# All curl commands in this guide use double quotes — required in cmd
 
# Option 2 — Stay in PowerShell but force the real curl binary
curl.exe -X POST "https://your-subdomain.authentication.us10.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/token" ^
  -H "Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded" ^
  -d "grant_type=client_credentials&client_id=YOUR_ID&client_secret=YOUR_SECRET"

All remaining examples in this guide use Command Prompt syntax with double quotes. If you prefer PowerShell, add .exe after curl and the commands will work identically.

ERROR 2 OF 6  
  ERROR 2    Curly quotes / bad characters    curl: (3) URL rejected: Port number was not a decimal number between 0 and 65535 
What You See
curl: (3) URL rejected: Port number was not a decimal number between 0 and 65535
What Causes It

When you copy a curl command from a web page, Teams message, email, or PDF, the quote characters are often converted to typographic curly quotes (“ ”) instead of straight ASCII quotes (" "). Curly quotes look identical on screen but are invisible corruption to curl — it cannot parse the URL correctly and reports a nonsensical port error.

This error is almost always a quote character issue. The URL itself is correct.

The Fix

Do not paste the URL from a rich text source. Type the URL manually, or paste into a plain text editor (Notepad, VS Code) first to strip formatting, then copy from there into the terminal.

Also ensure all quote marks in your command are straight ASCII double quotes (") — not curly (“”) or single (‘’). In Command Prompt, single quotes are not valid string delimiters at all.

# Wrong — curly quotes from copy-paste (looks fine, breaks silently)
curl -X POST “https://...authentication.../oauth/token”
 
# Correct — straight ASCII double quotes
curl -X POST "https://...authentication.../oauth/token"
ERROR 3 OF 6  
  ERROR 3    Single quotes in Command Prompt    curl: (3) URL rejected 
What You See
curl: (3) URL rejected: Port number was not a decimal number between 0 and 65535
What Causes It

Single quotes work as string delimiters in PowerShell and Linux/Mac terminals. They do not work in Windows Command Prompt. If you copy a curl command that uses single quotes (common in documentation written for Linux) and run it in cmd, Command Prompt interprets the single quote as part of the URL string — causing the same port parsing error as curly quotes.

The Fix

Replace all single quotes with double quotes when running curl in Command Prompt:

# Wrong — single quotes (Linux/Mac syntax, fails in Windows cmd)
curl -X POST 'https://...authentication.../oauth/token' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded'
 
# Correct — double quotes (Windows cmd syntax)
curl -X POST "https://...authentication.../oauth/token" ^
  -H "Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded"

In Command Prompt, line continuation uses ^ (caret) not \ (backslash). If you copy Linux curl commands with \ at the end of each line, remove them and replace with ^ or put the entire command on one line.

ERROR 4 OF 6  
  ERROR 4    Empty response — no output at all    Command exits with no output 
What You See

The curl command runs, the prompt returns, but nothing is printed. No error, no response, no JSON — just silence.

What Causes It

Three common causes in order of likelihood:

  • The -d parameter contains an & character that Command Prompt interprets as a command separator — splitting your command into two separate commands, one of which runs silently
  • A missing closing quote somewhere in the command causes cmd to wait for more input silently
  • The response is very large and the terminal is suppressing it — redirect to a file to capture it
The Fix

The & in client_credentials&client_id=... must stay inside double quotes. If it's outside quotes, Command Prompt treats it as a command separator:

# Wrong — & outside quotes splits the command
curl -X POST "https://.../oauth/token" -d grant_type=client_credentials&client_id=abc
 
# Correct — entire -d value inside double quotes
curl -X POST "https://.../oauth/token" ^
  -H "Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded" ^
  -d "grant_type=client_credentials&client_id=YOUR_ID&client_secret=YOUR_SECRET"

If you suspect a large response is being suppressed, redirect output to a file:

curl -X POST "https://.../oauth/token" ... > output.txt

Open output.txt — the full response will be there regardless of terminal display limitations.

ERROR 5 OF 6  
  ERROR 5    401 Unauthorized — missing /oauth/token path    {"error":"unauthorized"} 
What You See
{"error":"unauthorized","error_description":"An Authentication object was not found in the SecurityContext"}
What Causes It

The url field in your AI Core service key JSON is the base authentication domain — it does not include the /oauth/token path. Many practitioners use this URL directly and hit 401 because they're calling the base domain rather than the token endpoint.

Compare these two:

# What's in your service key url field (base domain — not the token endpoint)
https://your-subdomain.authentication.us10.hana.ondemand.com
 
# What you need to call (token endpoint — append /oauth/token manually)
https://your-subdomain.authentication.us10.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/token
The Fix

Always append /oauth/token to the url value from your service key. The SDK handles this automatically — but when calling directly with curl you must add it yourself:

curl -X POST "https://your-subdomain.authentication.us10.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/token" ^
  -H "Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded" ^
  -d "grant_type=client_credentials&client_id=YOUR_CLIENTID&client_secret=YOUR_SECRET"

A successful token call returns a JSON object containing access_token — a long base64-encoded string. This is your bearer token. Copy everything between the quotes after "access_token": and use it in all subsequent AI Core API calls. Tokens expire after 3600 seconds (1 hour) — generate a fresh one for each session.

ERROR 6 OF 6  
  ERROR 6    RBAC access denied on resource group creation    {"error":{"code":"403"}} 
What You See
{"error":{"code":"403","message":"RBAC: access denied"}}
What Causes It

RBAC access denied on a resource group PUT call means your service key does not have admin scope for this AI Core instance. Service keys are created with specific role assignments — a key created without aicore_admin scope can generate tokens and list resources but cannot create or modify resource groups.

This is not a token expiry issue and not a URL issue. The token is working — the credentials behind it simply don't have the permission required.

The Fix

First — check if a resource group already exists before trying to create one. Many AI Core instances provisioned by BTP have a default resource group already:

curl -X GET "https://YOUR_AI_API_URL/v2/admin/resourceGroups" ^
  -H "Authorization: Bearer YOUR_TOKEN"

If a resource group already exists with status PROVISIONED — use it. There is no need to create a new one.

If you genuinely need to create a new resource group and are hitting RBAC denial, recreate the service key with admin permissions:

  1. BTP Cockpit → Services → Instances → your AI Core instance → Service Keys
  2. Delete the existing service key
  3. Create a new service key — confirm aicore_admin role is included in the parameters
  4. Generate a new token using the new service key credentials

Check existing resource groups before attempting to create new ones. Most RBAC errors on resource group creation are unnecessary — the default resource group already exists and has status PROVISIONED. The GET call above takes 10 seconds and saves significant debugging time.

QUICK REFERENCE 

Authentication Error Quick Reference

 

Error / Symptom

Root Cause

Fix

ParameterBindingException — -X not recognized

PowerShell curl alias intercepting command

Use cmd or call curl.exe explicitly

Port number not decimal between 0 and 65535

Curly quotes from copy-paste corrupting URL

Type URL manually or paste through plain text editor first

Same port error with single quotes

Single quotes invalid in Windows cmd

Replace all single quotes with double quotes

No output — command exits silently

& in -d value treated as command separator by cmd

Keep entire -d value inside double quotes

401 — Authentication object not found

Calling base auth URL without /oauth/token suffix

Append /oauth/token to url value from service key

403 — RBAC access denied

Service key lacks aicore_admin scope

Check existing resource groups first — recreate key if creation is genuinely needed

THE WORKING COMMAND 

Complete Working Token Call for Windows Command Prompt

Once all six errors are resolved, this is the complete working token call. All values in capitals are replaced with your service key values:

curl -X POST "https://YOUR_SUBDOMAIN.authentication.us10.hana.ondemand.com/oauth/token" ^
  -H "Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded" ^
  -d "grant_type=client_credentials&client_id=YOUR_CLIENTID&client_secret=YOUR_SECRET"

A successful response looks like this — the access_token value is your bearer token:

{"access_token":"eyJhbGci...very long string...","token_type":"bearer","expires_in":3599}

Copy the full access_token value. In Part 2 we use it to call model deployments, diagnose 404 errors, resolve two-instance confusion, and fix resource group provisioning failures.

If your response contains access_token — authentication is complete. Every error in this article is behind you. Part 2 covers everything from this point to a working model response.

entry.title: Grounding AI in Reality: How We Made Joule Answer From Our Own Documents
entry.content:
There’s a moment in most AI projects where you realize the model confidently gave you a completely wrong answer — and you have no idea how to fix it. You can’t retrain it, you can’t shout at it, and you definitely can’t ship it to users like that.

That was roughly where we were when we started experimenting with Document Grounding on SAP BTP. We wanted Joule (SAP’s AI assistant) to answer questions from a specific equipment manual — not from its general training data, but from our actual PDF. This post is about how we got there.

What We Were Trying to Do

The goal was simple on paper: take a technical PDF (an operator manual for a loader), upload it to SAP’s Document Grounding service, and let Joule search through it when users ask questions. Instead of guessing or hallucinating, Joule would pull actual passages from the document and answer based on those.

The underlying pattern is called RAG — Retrieval-Augmented Generation. You store your documents as vectors (mathematical representations of meaning), and when a question comes in, you find the most semantically relevant chunks and feed them to the LLM as context. It’s become the standard way to make AI useful on private knowledge bases.

SAP BTP has its own managed infrastructure for this through the Document Grounding service, and there’s a Python library called langchain-rage that wraps its API in a LangChain-compatible interface.

Prerequisites

Before you try to replicate this, you’ll need:

  • An SAP BTP account with the Document Grounding service instance provisioned
  • Identity service set up with X.509 certificate support (this is what handles authentication)
  • Service keys generated from both the Document Grounding service and the Identity service — you’ll need these as JSON files locally
  • Python 3.11+ — we used 3.11.14
  • A PDF you want to search — for our test we used a 114-page equipment manual
  • cf CLI access to pull fresh service keys if needed
  • Joule Studio access if you want to test the end-to-end conversational integration

How the Pieces Fit Together

Before diving into setup, here’s the high-level picture:

Your PDF
   ↓
Load pages with PyMuPDF
   ↓
Split into ~1000-character chunks (LangChain splitter)
   ↓
Upload to BTP Document Grounding (vector store)
   ↓
BTP auto-generates embeddings for each chunk
   ↓
User asks a question in Joule or your app
   ↓
Semantic search finds the most relevant chunks
   ↓
LLM generates a grounded, cited answer

The service handles the embedding generation — you don’t need to bring your own embedding model or manage any of that infrastructure. That’s honestly one of the nicer aspects of this approach.

Setting Up the Environment

# Create and activate a virtual environment
python -m venv .venv
source .venv/bin/activate

# Install dependencies
pip install langchain-community langchain-openai pymupdf requests-toolbelt

# Install the SAP RAGe LangChain integration (from internal git)
pip install git+https://<your-internal-repo>/langchain-rage.git

Place your service key files in the project root: - doc-grounding-service-key-fresh.json — from your Document Grounding service instance - identity-key-x509-fresh.json — from your Identity service instance (must include X.509 certs)

If your service keys are more than a day or two old, regenerate them via CF CLI. Stale keys were a source of several confusing authentication failures for us.

Step 1: Create the Collection

A “collection” is the vector store bucket that holds your document chunks. The key thing to get right is the metadata — specifically setting type=custom. Without this, Joule won’t discover the collection when grounding responses.

from langchain_rage.rage_clients.vector.client import VectorClient

vector_client = VectorClient.create_from_service_keys(
    path_document_grounding_key="doc-grounding-service-key-fresh.json",
    path_identity_key="identity-key-x509-fresh.json"
)

# Create the collection
collection = vector_client.create_collection(
    title="my-document-collection",
    metadata={"type": "custom"}  # Required for Joule integration
)

collection_id = collection.id
print(f"Collection created: {collection_id}")

Save that collection ID somewhere — you’ll reference it in every subsequent operation.

 

Step 2: Ingest Your PDF

This is where most of the work happens. We load the PDF with PyMuPDF (which preserves page numbers in metadata — important for citations), split into chunks, convert to the RAGe format, and upload.

from langchain_community.document_loaders import DirectoryLoader, PyMuPDFLoader
from langchain.text_splitter import RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter
from langchain_rage.rage_clients.vector.model import (
    Chunk as RAGeChunk,
    Document as RAGeDocument,
    MetaData as RAGeMetaData
)
import time

# Load
loader = DirectoryLoader(path=".", glob="*.pdf", loader_cls=PyMuPDFLoader)
raw_documents = loader.load()

# Split
chunker = RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter(
    chunk_size=1000,
    chunk_overlap=100
)

# Convert and upload in batches
batch_size = 50
all_ids = []

for raw_doc in raw_documents:
    chunks = chunker.split_documents([raw_doc])
    rage_docs = []

    for chunk in chunks:
        rage_chunk = RAGeChunk(
            content=chunk.page_content,
            metadata=[
                RAGeMetaData(key=k, value=[str(v)])
                for k, v in chunk.metadata.items()
            ]
        )
        rage_doc = RAGeDocument(
            chunks=[rage_chunk],
            metadata=[RAGeMetaData(key="title", value=[raw_doc.metadata["source"]])]
        )
        rage_docs.append(rage_doc)

    # Upload in batches
    for i in range(0, len(rage_docs), batch_size):
        batch = rage_docs[i:i + batch_size]
        try:
            ids = vector_client.create_documents(
                collection_id=collection_id,
                documents=batch
            )
            all_ids.extend(ids)
            time.sleep(1)  # Be nice to the stage environment
        except Exception as e:
            print(f"Batch failed: {e}")
            continue

print(f"Uploaded {len(all_ids)} chunks")

About chunk sizing: We settled on 1000 characters with 100 character overlap. Smaller chunks give more precise retrieval but miss broader context; larger chunks are the opposite. 1000 works well for technical documentation with a mix of lists, tables, and prose. You’ll likely need to tune this for your content type.

About batching: If you’re on stage/canary, do not try to upload everything in one shot. We lost entire uploads that way. Batches of 50 with a 1-second delay between them worked reliably. If you’re resuming a partial upload, drop to batches of 10 and add a 3-second delay.

For a 114-page PDF, we ended up with 233 chunks total. The whole upload took a few minutes with batching.

Step 3: Verify the Collection

Before moving on, it’s worth checking that the upload actually worked:

status = vector_client.get_collection(collection_id=collection_id)
print(f"Collection status: {status}")
You can also run a quick semantic query to verify the chunks are searchable:
from langchain_rage.rage_clients.retrieval.client import RetrievalClient
from langchain_rage.rage_clients.retrieval.model import Search, SearchFilter, SearchConfiguration

retrieval_client = RetrievalClient.create_from_service_keys(
    path_document_grounding_key="doc-grounding-service-key-fresh.json",
    path_identity_key="identity-key-x509-fresh.json"
)

search = Search(
    query="maintenance intervals",
    filters=[SearchFilter(
        dataRepositories=[collection_id],
        dataRepositoryType="vector",
        searchConfiguration=SearchConfiguration(maxChunkCount=3)
    )]
)

results = retrieval_client.query(search).json()

If you get chunks back with relevance scores in the 0.4–0.6 range, you’re in good shape. Scores above 0.5 on a meaningful query indicate solid embedding quality.

Step 4: Joule Integration

Once your collection is live, connecting it to Joule requires a deployment through Joule Studio. The key configuration is the capability definition — specifically making sure the capability points to your collection and is marked with the right metadata so Joule’s grounding layer can discover it.

The deployment manifest structure looks roughly like:

# da.sapdas.yaml (simplified)
apiVersion: sapdas/v1
kind: DeploymentAssistant
metadata:
  name: my-document-grounding-capability
spec:
  capabilities:
    - path: a2a/
# a2a/capability.sapdas.yaml (simplified)
apiVersion: sapdas/v3
kind: Capability
metadata:
  name: document-search
spec:
  grounding:
    collections:
      - id: "<your-collection-id>"
        type: custom

After deploying via Joule Studio’s CLI, Joule will automatically use your collection when users ask questions that match its scope. The grounding is transparent — users just see answers, and Joule cites the source pages.

What It Looks Like in Practice

Once everything is connected, the experience is straightforward. A user asks Joule something like “what’s the maintenance schedule for the first 100 operating hours?” — Joule runs a semantic search against the collection, finds the relevant passages (in our case from pages 62 and 76–77 of the manual), and generates a response grounded in those chunks.

The relevance scores we saw in testing:

Query

Score Range

Source Pages

“maintenance intervals”

0.50 – 0.56

62, 76–77

“safety requirements before operation”

0.45 – 0.50

18

“engine oil change procedure”

0.50 – 0.55

76–77

“technical specifications”

0.48 – 0.54

30

Not all queries will score high — if a topic isn’t covered in your document, scores drop noticeably (below 0.3) and Joule will typically indicate it couldn’t find relevant information. That failure mode is actually what you want: grounded AI that knows what it doesn’t know.

 

 

A Few Things We’d Do Differently

Start with a resume-capable ingestion script. We wrote ingest_pdf.py first (one big upload), then ingest_pdf_batched.py (batched), then ingest_pdf_resume.py (resume from checkpoint). We should have started with the third one. Save your uploaded document IDs to a file as you go, and on retry, skip chunks that already made it through.

Test semantic search before deploying to Joule. We spent time debugging the Joule integration when the actual problem was that some chunks didn’t upload. A simple retrieval test catches this early.

Keep your service keys fresh. X.509 certificates have expiry dates. If you hit sudden authentication failures days later, regenerating keys is the first thing to try.

Name your collections descriptively. We used joule-document-test which was fine for a PoC but would get confusing fast in a real environment with multiple document sets.

Wrapping Up

Document grounding is one of those features where the demo works immediately and then you discover all the small things that make it production-ready: batch handling, error recovery, chunk size tuning, collection discoverability. The SAP BTP Document Grounding service handles the hard parts (embedding generation, vector storage, similarity search) reasonably well — the langchain-rage integration makes it approachable from Python without dealing with raw API calls.

If you’re building anything where you need AI to answer reliably from a known corpus — internal documentation, product manuals, compliance documents — this pattern is worth understanding. It’s not magic, but it’s close enough.

Setup based on SAP BTP Document Grounding service . The langchain-rage library is an internal SAP integration library for LangChain. Joule Studio is required for the conversational integration step.

entry.title: SAP Agentic AI in Practice: Concepts, Architecture, and Your First LangGraph Agent (Part 1 of 6)
entry.content:
SAP COMMUNITY  |  SERIES 2: SAP AGENTIC AI IN PRACTICE  |  PART 1 OF 6
SAP Agentic AI in Practice: Concepts, Architecture, and Your First LangGraph Agent

What agentic AI actually means in SAP contexts, when to use an agent vs a skill vs a pipeline, the full agentic SAP stack with LangGraph and AI Core, and a working ReAct agent for SAP data — all in Part 1.

By Deepa Dorairaj   |  SAP AI Solution Architect  |  Series 2, Part 1 of 6  |  Published 2026

TL;DR: Agentic AI is not just a more capable pipeline. It's a fundamentally different execution model — the agent decides what to do next based on what it observes, rather than following a fixed sequence. This article covers what that means for SAP workloads specifically, when agents are the right choice vs skills and pipelines, the complete LangGraph + AI Core architecture for production SAP agents, and a working ReAct agent that queries SAP data, analyzes it, and produces findings autonomously.

Series 2 Context — Building on the Generative AI Hub Foundation

This is Part 1 of SAP Agentic AI in Practice — the second series building on the Generative AI Hub foundation. Series 1 covered setup, Python SDK, prompt pipelines, RAG on HANA Cloud, CAP applications, and Joule Studio. If you haven't read that series, the architecture knowledge from Parts 3–5 will be particularly useful background for this one.

Series 2 moves from pipelines to agents. The distinction matters — and Part 1 establishes it clearly before writing a single line of agent code. The four SAP use cases this series builds toward are: data migration landscape assessment, exception log triage, master data quality, and S/4HANA readiness. Each becomes a specialized agent. By Part 6 they work together as a coordinated multi-agent system.

PART A: CONCEPTS — WHAT AGENTIC AI ACTUALLY MEANS 

Agent vs Skill vs Pipeline — The Distinction That Matters

These three terms are used interchangeably in most SAP AI conversations. They shouldn't be. They represent fundamentally different execution models with different appropriate use cases.

Type

How It Works

SAP Example

Prompt Pipeline

Fixed sequence of prompts — output of one feeds the next. You define every step upfront. The model executes but doesn't decide.

Exception log triage with defined classify → summarize → recommend steps. Predictable, auditable, fast.

Joule Skill

Structured, deterministic capability triggered by user intent. Calls a specific API action. No reasoning or planning involved.

Answering a knowledge base question, looking up a material, creating a badge. Single-purpose, reliable.

AI Agent

Autonomous reasoner. Given a goal, the agent decides which tools to use, in what order, based on what it observes at each step. The sequence is not predetermined.

Assess an SAP landscape for S/4HANA readiness — the agent decides which systems to query, what to look for, and how to structure findings based on what it discovers.

The key differentiator is decision-making under uncertainty. Pipelines and skills are deterministic — given the same input, they always follow the same path. Agents are adaptive — they observe, reason, and decide what to do next based on what they find.

When to Use Each

Use a pipeline when the steps are known upfront and the output structure is fixed. Use a skill when the task is focused and the API call is well-defined. Use an agent when the task requires exploration, multi-step reasoning, or when the path to completion depends on what the agent discovers along the way.

Scenario

Use

SAP Example

Known steps, fixed output structure

Prompt pipeline

Exception log triage, document extraction

Single focused task, defined API

Joule skill

Material lookup, KB question, badge creation

Goal-directed, path unknown upfront

Agent

Landscape assessment, readiness analysis

Multi-step with dependencies on findings

Agent

Data quality sweep, migration risk scoring

Needs human approval at checkpoints

Agent with human-in-the-loop

Transport risk assessment, SoD conflict review

Agents are not always better than pipelines. They are more expensive (more LLM calls), less predictable (the path varies), and harder to debug. Use agents specifically when the task requires adaptive reasoning. For everything else, pipelines and skills are faster, cheaper, and more reliable.

PART B: ARCHITECTURE — THE AGENTIC SAP STACK 

The Complete LangGraph + AI Core Architecture for SAP Agents

Production SAP agents require four layers working together. LangGraph provides the agent orchestration framework. SAP AI Core provides governed foundation model access. HANA Cloud provides the data and vector layer. BTP Cloud Foundry provides the deployment and runtime environment.

LangGraph Agent → AI Core (foundation model) → SAP Tools (HANA, APIs) → HANA vector store → Findings output
Why LangGraph for SAP Agents

LangGraph is a graph-based agent orchestration framework built on LangChain. It models agent execution as a directed graph — nodes are actions or decisions, edges are transitions. This architecture makes it uniquely suited for SAP workloads for three reasons:

  • State persistence — LangGraph maintains agent state explicitly across steps. For SAP assessments that query multiple systems and accumulate findings, state persistence is essential.
  • Conditional routing — edges in the graph can be conditional. An agent can take different paths based on what it finds — escalate a critical finding, skip a check if a prerequisite fails, loop back for more data if confidence is low.
  • Human-in-the-loop support — LangGraph has native support for pausing execution and waiting for human approval before proceeding. For SAP processes where autonomous action carries risk, this is not optional.
The ReAct Pattern

ReAct (Reason + Act) is the foundational agent pattern used throughout this series. The agent alternates between two steps:

  • Reason — the model thinks about what it knows and what it needs to do next
  • Act — the model calls a tool (a function, an API, a database query) and observes the result

This cycle continues until the agent has enough information to produce a final answer or reaches a defined stopping condition. For SAP workloads, the tools are SAP-specific: HANA queries, RFC calls, AI Core model invocations, vector store retrievals.

Goal input → Reason: what do I know? → Act: call SAP tool → Observe result → Reason: what next? → Final findings
Agent State Schema for SAP Assessments

Every LangGraph agent requires an explicit state schema. For SAP assessment agents, the state carries the accumulated findings, queries run, systems checked, and confidence scores across all steps:

# Agent state schema — carries context across all reasoning steps

from typing import TypedDict, List, Optional

from langgraph.graph import StateGraph, END

class SAPAssessmentState(TypedDict):

    goal:              str          # What the agent is trying to assess

    system_info:       dict         # SAP system metadata collected so far

    findings:          List[dict]   # Accumulated findings with severity

    queries_run:       List[str]    # Queries executed — for audit trail

    tools_used:        List[str]    # Tools called — for transparency

    confidence_score:  float        # Overall confidence in findings

    next_action:       str          # What the agent decided to do next

    human_review_needed: bool       # Flag for human-in-the-loop checkpoint

    final_report:      Optional[str] # Final output when complete
PART C: YOUR FIRST SAP AGENT — REACT WITH LANGGRAPH 

Building a ReAct Agent for SAP Data Assessment

This first agent demonstrates the complete ReAct pattern on a focused SAP task: given a HANA table name, autonomously assess its data quality — checking volume, null rates, duplicate rates, and anomalies — and produce a structured findings report. This is the foundation pattern that the more complex agents in Parts 2–6 build on.

Step 1 — Install Dependencies
# Install LangGraph and SAP AI SDK
pip install langgraph langchain-core generative-ai-hub-sdk hdbcli
Step 2 — Define SAP Tools

Tools are the functions the agent can call. Each tool has a name, description (used by the model to decide when to call it), and implementation. For SAP agents, tools wrap HANA queries and AI Core calls:

# tools.py — SAP tools available to the agent
from langchain_core.tools import tool
import hdbcli.dbapi as hdb
import json
 
# HANA connection — credentials from environment or .aicore-config.json
def get_hana_connection():
    return hdb.connect(
        address=os.environ['HANA_HOST'],
        port=443,
        user=os.environ['HANA_USER'],
        password=os.environ['HANA_PASSWORD'],
        encrypt=True
    )
 
@tool
def get_table_volume(table_name: str) -> str:
    """Get the row count and basic metadata for an SAP HANA table."""
    conn = get_hana_connection()
    cursor = conn.cursor()
    cursor.execute(f'SELECT COUNT(*) FROM {table_name}')
    count = cursor.fetchone()[0]
    return json.dumps({'table': table_name, 'row_count': count})
 
@tool
def get_null_rate(table_name: str, column_name: str) -> str:
    """Calculate the null rate for a specific column in an SAP HANA table."""
    conn = get_hana_connection()
    cursor = conn.cursor()
    cursor.execute(f'''
        SELECT
            COUNT(*) as total,
            SUM(CASE WHEN {column_name} IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) as nulls,
            ROUND(100.0 * SUM(CASE WHEN {column_name} IS NULL THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)
                / COUNT(*), 2) as null_rate_pct
        FROM {table_name}
    ''')
    row = cursor.fetchone()
    return json.dumps({
        'table': table_name, 'column': column_name,
        'total': row[0], 'nulls': row[1], 'null_rate_pct': float(row[2])
    })
 
@tool
def get_duplicate_rate(table_name: str, key_column: str) -> str:
    """Identify duplicate records based on a key column in an SAP HANA table."""
    conn = get_hana_connection()
    cursor = conn.cursor()
    cursor.execute(f'''
        SELECT COUNT(*) - COUNT(DISTINCT {key_column}) as duplicate_count,
               COUNT(*) as total_count
        FROM {table_name}
    ''')
    row = cursor.fetchone()
    return json.dumps({
        'table': table_name, 'key_column': key_column,
        'duplicate_count': row[0], 'total_count': row[1],
        'duplicate_rate_pct': round(100.0 * row[0] / row[1], 2) if row[1] > 0 else 0
    })
 
@tool
def generate_assessment_report(findings: str) -> str:
    """Generate a structured data quality assessment report from accumulated findings."""
    from gen_ai_hub.proxy.langchain.openai import ChatOpenAI
    from langchain.schema import HumanMessage, SystemMessage
 
    llm = ChatOpenAI(proxy_model_name='gpt-4o')
    response = llm([
        SystemMessage(content='''You are an SAP data quality expert.
        Generate a structured assessment report from the findings provided.
        Include: Executive Summary, Critical Issues, Recommendations.
        Be specific and actionable.'''),
        HumanMessage(content=f'Generate report from findings:\n{findings}')
    ])
    return response.content

Step 3 — Build the LangGraph Agent

# agent.py — LangGraph ReAct agent for SAP data quality assessment
from langgraph.prebuilt import create_react_agent
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.langchain.openai import ChatOpenAI
from tools import get_table_volume, get_null_rate, get_duplicate_rate, generate_assessment_report
 
# Initialize foundation model via SAP AI Core
llm = ChatOpenAI(proxy_model_name='gpt-4o')
 
# Register SAP tools the agent can use
tools = [get_table_volume, get_null_rate, get_duplicate_rate, generate_assessment_report]
 
# System prompt — defines agent behavior and boundaries
system_prompt = '''You are an SAP data quality assessment agent.
Your goal is to autonomously assess the data quality of SAP HANA tables.

For each assessment:
1. Check the table volume first
2. Check null rates for key columns
3. Check duplicate rates on primary key columns
4. Flag any issue where null_rate > 5% or duplicate_rate > 0.1% as HIGH severity
5. Generate a structured findings report when assessment is complete

Always explain your reasoning before calling a tool.
Always summarize what you found after each tool call.
Stop when you have assessed all requested dimensions.'''
 
# Create the ReAct agent — LangGraph handles the reason/act loop
agent = create_react_agent(
    model=llm,
    tools=tools,
    state_modifier=system_prompt
)

Step 4 — Run the Agent

# Run the agent on an SAP table assessment task
from langchain_core.messages import HumanMessage
 
result = agent.invoke({
    'messages': [HumanMessage(content='''
        Assess the data quality of table MARA (SAP material master).
        Check volume, null rate on MTART and MATKL columns,
        and duplicate rate on MATNR. Generate a findings report.
    ''')]
})
 
# Print the agent's final response
print(result['messages'][-1].content)

The agent will reason through the task step by step — calling get_table_volume first, then get_null_rate for each column, then get_duplicate_rate, and finally generate_assessment_report with all accumulated findings. The reasoning trace is visible in the messages — each HumanMessage and AIMessage shows exactly what the agent thought and did.

What the Agent Output Looks Like

The agent produces a structured findings report with:

  • Executive Summary — overall data quality score and critical issues count
  • Findings per dimension — volume, nulls, duplicates with actual values
  • Severity classification — Critical, High, Medium, Low per finding
  • Specific recommendations — actionable fixes referencing actual column names and values

This output is the foundation for the migration assessment agent in Part 2, which extends this pattern across multiple SAP tables and produces a full landscape readiness report.

╔══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
║         SAP DATA QUALITY ASSESSMENT AGENT — EXECUTION TRACE      ║
║         Table: MARA (Material Master — General Data)             ║
╚══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════╝

[THOUGHT] I need to begin by checking the volume of the MARA table to
understand the scale of the assessment before evaluating data quality
dimensions.

[ACTION] get_table_volume("MARA")
[OBSERVATION] {"table": "MARA", "row_count": 847293}

[THOUGHT] The table contains 847,293 material master records. This is
a significant volume — quality issues even at low percentages will
affect thousands of records. I'll now check null rates starting with
MTART (Material Type), a critical classification field.

[ACTION] get_null_rate("MARA", "MTART")
[OBSERVATION] {"table": "MARA", "column": "MTART", "total": 847293,
               "nulls": 169, "null_rate_pct": 0.02}

[THOUGHT] MTART null rate is 0.02% — acceptable. 169 records without
a material type is low risk. Moving to MATKL (Material Group), which
drives procurement categorization and reporting.

[ACTION] get_null_rate("MARA", "MATKL")
[OBSERVATION] {"table": "MARA", "column": "MATKL", "total": 847293,
               "nulls": 73,969, "null_rate_pct": 8.73}

[THOUGHT] MATKL null rate is 8.73% — this exceeds the 5% threshold
and is flagged HIGH severity. 73,969 materials without a material group
will cause issues in procurement reporting, spend analytics, and any
AI classification pipeline that relies on MATKL for categorization.
This needs to be highlighted in the final report.

[ACTION] get_duplicate_rate("MARA", "MATNR")
[OBSERVATION] {"table": "MARA", "key_column": "MATNR",
               "duplicate_count": 0, "total_count": 847293,
               "duplicate_rate_pct": 0.0}

[THOUGHT] MATNR (Material Number) has zero duplicates — primary key
integrity is intact. I now have sufficient findings to generate the
structured assessment report.

[ACTION] generate_assessment_report(findings)

════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
SAP DATA QUALITY ASSESSMENT REPORT
Table: MARA — Material Master General Data
Assessment Date: 2026-03-28 | Agent: SAP Data Quality Agent v1.0
════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

EXECUTIVE SUMMARY
─────────────────
Overall Data Quality Score: 74/100 — ATTENTION REQUIRED
Records Assessed: 847,293
Critical Issues: 0  |  High Severity: 1  |  Low Severity: 2

One high-severity data quality issue was identified that will impact
procurement reporting, spend analytics, and downstream AI pipelines
that rely on material group classification. Immediate remediation is
recommended before any S/4HANA migration or AI workload deployment.

FINDINGS
────────
┌──────────────┬──────────────┬───────────────┬──────────┬──────────┐
│ Dimension    │ Column       │ Value         │ Threshold│ Severity │
├──────────────┼──────────────┼───────────────┼──────────┼──────────┤
│ Volume       │ —            │ 847,293 rows  │ —        │ OK       │
│ Null Rate    │ MTART        │ 0.02% (169)   │ < 5%     │ LOW      │
│ Null Rate    │ MATKL        │ 8.73% (73,969)│ < 5%     │ HIGH ⚠   │
│ Duplicates   │ MATNR        │ 0.0%          │ < 0.1%   │ LOW      │
└──────────────┴──────────────┴───────────────┴──────────┴──────────┘

HIGH SEVERITY FINDING — MATKL (Material Group) Null Rate: 8.73%
73,969 of 847,293 material records are missing a Material Group
assignment. Impact areas:
  • Procurement spend reporting by category will be incomplete
  • AI-based material classification will produce unreliable results
  • S/4HANA migration data validation will flag these records

RECOMMENDATIONS
───────────────
1. [IMMEDIATE] Run MATKL gap analysis — extract all MATNR records
   where MATKL is null. Group by MTART to identify which material
   types are most affected. This scopes the remediation effort.

2. [SHORT-TERM] Assign Material Groups using the AI classification
   pipeline (Part 2 of Generative AI Hub series) — embed existing
   classified materials as reference examples and classify the
   73,969 unclassified records using RAG-augmented classification.

3. [BEFORE MIGRATION] Validate MATKL completeness reaches < 2%
   null rate before proceeding with any S/4HANA migration data
   load. Include this check in your migration go/no-go criteria.

4. [PROCESS] Add MATKL as a required field in material master
   creation workflows in MM01 to prevent future gaps.

════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
Agent completed assessment in 4 reasoning steps | 4 tool calls
Confidence Score: 0.91 | Human Review Required: NO
════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
SERIES 2 ROADMAP 

What This Series Builds Across 6 Parts

 

Part

Title

What It Builds

Part 1

Concepts, Architecture, First ReAct Agent

Agent vs skill vs pipeline, LangGraph + AI Core stack, data quality ReAct agent on HANA

Part 2

SAP Data Migration Assessment Agent

Multi-tool agent that analyzes SAP landscape, scores migration readiness, produces findings report

Part 3

SAP Exception Log Triage Agent

Autonomous root cause analysis — agent queries logs, identifies patterns, recommends actions without human triage

Part 4

SAP Master Data Quality Agent

Detects data issues across master data domains, prioritizes fixes, generates remediation plan

Part 5

S/4HANA Readiness Agent

Assesses custom code and configuration against clean core principles, flags migration blockers

Part 6

Multi-Agent Orchestration

All four agents working together — an orchestrator agent delegates to specialized sub-agents and synthesizes a unified SAP landscape report

What's Next — Part 2

Part 2 builds the SAP Data Migration Assessment Agent — extending the ReAct pattern from a single table to a full landscape assessment across multiple systems, with conditional routing based on findings and a scored readiness report as output. The agent decides autonomously which systems to query, what to look for, and how to weight findings based on migration impact.

If you work through the ReAct agent in this article and want to extend it to your own SAP tables — drop a comment with your use case. The pattern generalizes across any HANA table and any assessment dimension. The tool definitions are the only thing that changes.

entry.title: SAP Agentic AI in Practice: Building the SAP Migration Assessment Agent (Part 2 of 6)
entry.content:
SAP COMMUNITY  |  SERIES 2: SAP AGENTIC AI IN PRACTICE  |  PART 2 OF 6

SAP Agentic AI in Practice: Building the SAP Migration Assessment Agent

From a single ReAct agent to a full multi-tool LangGraph state machine with human-in-the-loop — assessing custom code health, table complexity, configuration footprint, and integration dependencies to produce a scored S/4HANA migration readiness report.

By Deepa Dorairaj   |  SAP AI Solution Architect  |  Series 2, Part 2 of 6  |  Published 2026

TL;DR: Part 2 builds the SAP Migration Assessment Agent in two stages. Stage A extends the Part 1 ReAct pattern into a single agent with human-in-the-loop that assesses all four migration dimensions — custom code, table complexity, configuration footprint, and integration dependencies. Stage B evolves this into a LangGraph state machine with conditional routing, where the agent's path through the assessment changes based on what it finds. Both stages produce a scored migration readiness report with dimension-level findings.

Series Context

This is Part 2 of SAP Agentic AI in Practice. Part 1 established the foundational concepts — agent vs skill vs pipeline, the ReAct pattern, LangGraph architecture, and a first working agent for SAP data quality assessment. If you haven't read Part 1, the agent state schema and tool definitions introduced there are the starting point for everything built here.

Part 2 applies those foundations to a real SAP migration use case — assessing an SAP landscape for S/4HANA readiness across four dimensions. This is the kind of assessment that currently takes SAP migration teams weeks of manual analysis. The agent does it autonomously, with a human-in-the-loop checkpoint before the final report is produced.

WHY MIGRATION ASSESSMENT IS AN IDEAL AGENT USE CASE 

Why Migration Assessment Fits the Agent Pattern

S/4HANA migration assessment is one of the most time-consuming and inconsistently executed activities in SAP consulting. Two experienced consultants assessing the same landscape often produce different findings because the process is manual, undocumented, and dependent on tribal knowledge about what to look for.

It fits the agent pattern for three reasons:

  • The path is not predetermined — what the agent looks for in custom code depends on how much custom code exists. What it investigates in integrations depends on which adapters it finds. The assessment adapts to the landscape.
  • It requires multi-tool reasoning — no single query answers the readiness question. The agent must combine findings across custom code, data volume, configuration, and interfaces to produce a meaningful score.
  • Human review before action is natural — a migration readiness report should be reviewed by a consultant before being shared with a client. Human-in-the-loop isn't a constraint here, it's the correct workflow.

Assessment Dimension

What It Signals for Migration

Custom code health

Volume of Z-objects, modifications, and deprecated API usage — signals clean core compliance gap

Table complexity

Record volumes and growth rates across key business objects — signals data migration effort

Configuration footprint

Scope of customizing across modules — signals business process complexity and testing effort

Integration dependencies

Interface count, adapter types, and external system connections — signals technical migration risk

STAGE A: SINGLE AGENT WITH HUMAN-IN-THE-LOOP 

Stage A — Extending the ReAct Pattern with Human-in-the-Loop

Stage A extends the Part 1 ReAct agent into a migration assessment agent. The core pattern is the same — the agent reasons and acts in a loop using SAP-specific tools. Two things are added: all four assessment dimensions as tools, and a human-in-the-loop checkpoint before the final report is generated.

Goal: assess landscape → Custom code check → Table complexity → Config footprint → Integration scan → Human review → Scored report
Assessment State Schema

Extend the SAPAssessmentState from Part 1 with migration-specific fields:

# migration_agent.py — extends Part 1 state schema
from typing import TypedDict, List, Optional, Dict
from langgraph.graph import StateGraph, END
 
class MigrationAssessmentState(TypedDict):
    goal:                str
    system_id:           str          # SAP system being assessed
    # Dimension findings
    custom_code:         Optional[dict]  # Z-objects, mods, deprecated APIs
    table_complexity:    Optional[dict]  # Volumes and growth rates
    config_footprint:    Optional[dict]  # Customizing scope per module
    integration_deps:    Optional[dict]  # Interface count and types
    # Scoring
    dimension_scores:    Dict[str, int]   # Score per dimension 0-100
    overall_score:       Optional[int]    # Weighted overall readiness score
    readiness_level:     Optional[str]    # HIGH / MEDIUM / LOW / CRITICAL
    # Control
    findings:            List[dict]
    human_review_needed: bool
    human_approved:      bool
    final_report:        Optional[str]
Migration Assessment Tools
# tools/migration_tools.py
from langchain_core.tools import tool
import hdbcli.dbapi as hdb, json, os
 
def get_conn():
    return hdb.connect(address=os.environ['HANA_HOST'], port=443,
                       user=os.environ['HANA_USER'],
                       password=os.environ['HANA_PASSWORD'], encrypt=True)
 
@tool
def assess_custom_code(system_id: str) -> str:
    """Assess custom code health — count Z-objects, modifications,
    and usage of deprecated APIs that block S/4HANA migration."""
    conn = get_conn()
    cursor = conn.cursor()
 
    # Count Z-programs and includes
    cursor.execute('''
        SELECT COUNT(*) as z_program_count
        FROM TADIR
        WHERE DEVCLASS LIKE 'Z%' AND OBJECT IN ('PROG', 'FUGR', 'CLAS')
    ''')
    z_count = cursor.fetchone()[0]
 
    # Count SAP standard modifications (risky for migration)
    cursor.execute('''
        SELECT COUNT(*) as mod_count
        FROM TADIR
        WHERE DEVCLASS NOT LIKE 'Z%'
        AND DEVCLASS NOT LIKE '$%'
        AND DEVCLASS != 'LOCAL'
        AND OBJECT IN ('PROG', 'FUGR', 'CLAS', 'DOMA', 'DTEL')
    ''')
    mod_count = cursor.fetchone()[0]
 
    return json.dumps({
        'z_program_count': z_count,
        'modification_count': mod_count,
        'clean_core_risk': 'HIGH' if mod_count > 100 else
                           'MEDIUM' if mod_count > 20 else 'LOW'
    })
 
@tool
def assess_table_complexity(system_id: str) -> str:
    """Assess data volume complexity across key business object tables."""
    conn = get_conn()
    cursor = conn.cursor()
 
    # Key tables that drive migration effort
    key_tables = ['BKPF', 'BSEG', 'VBAK', 'VBAP', 'EKKO',
                  'EKPO', 'MARA', 'KNA1', 'LFA1', 'AUFK']
    volumes = {}
 
    for table in key_tables:
        try:
            cursor.execute(f'SELECT COUNT(*) FROM {table}')
            volumes[table] = cursor.fetchone()[0]
        except Exception:
            volumes[table] = 'unavailable'
 
    total_records = sum(v for v in volumes.values() if isinstance(v, int))
    return json.dumps({
        'table_volumes': volumes,
        'total_key_records': total_records,
        'volume_risk': 'HIGH' if total_records > 500_000_000 else
                       'MEDIUM' if total_records > 50_000_000 else 'LOW'
    })
 
@tool
def assess_config_footprint(system_id: str) -> str:
